question: Compare different approaches to adding memory in an AI support agent.
answer: There are six primary approaches to adding memory in an AI support agent — In-Context/Sliding Window, Vector Store (RAG), Summarization/Compression, Knowledge Graph, Fine-Tuning, and Hybrid/Layered — each with distinct trade-offs in continuity, latency, cost, scalability, and complexity. No single approach is universally optimal: production systems in 2026 increasingly converge on hybrid architectures that combine short-term context windows, RAG for knowledge breadth, and persistent memory stores for personalization and cross-session continuity.

key_findings.claim: In-context (sliding window) memory is zero-latency and zero-cost but volatile — lost at session end — and performance drops 39% on average from single-turn to multi-turn interactions across 15 LLMs tested by Microsoft Research and Salesforce.

Vector store (RAG-based) memory retrieves semantically relevant fragments from large corpora via cosine similarity; RAG retrieval accuracy runs 85–92% on well-governed data but drops to 45–60% with ungoverned data, and roughly 40% of RAG failures trace to data quality issues.

Naively appending every conversation turn into a vector database leads to retrieval noise, context dilution, and latency spikes over time — effective long-term memory requires sophisticated orchestration for consolidation, scoring, and forgetting.

Summarization/compression memory periodically condenses transcripts into rolling summaries and entity-centric profiles, capping storage growth; enterprise-grade architectures rely on asynchronous background consolidation to avoid session latency overhead.

Knowledge graph memory (e.g., Zep's Graphiti) organizes memories as nodes and relationships with temporal metadata (created_at, valid_at, expired_at), providing bi-temporal awareness and achieving strong accuracy improvements over full-context baselines on agent memory benchmarks.

Fine-tuning (parametric memory) bakes knowledge into model weights, is best for style/tone adaptation, but crystallizes knowledge at a point in time — any update requires a full retraining cycle taking weeks, and produces outputs with no traceable source.

Hybrid/layered memory combines hot memory (context window), warm memory (structured facts in fast databases for RAG), and cold archives (compressed logs for compliance), with a customer support copilot using working memory for current query, episodic memory for prior interactions, and semantic memory for entitlement verification.

The field is converging on hybrid architectures combining factual long-term memory, working short-term context, and RAG for corpus lookup — most production agentic systems use both RAG (for knowledge breadth) and memory (for personalization and session continuity).

limitations: Latency benchmarks for knowledge graph retrieval were not directly found in sources.
Cost figures for hybrid architectures depend heavily on implementation choices and are not standardized.
Scalability data for summarization-only approaches in very large-scale deployments was limited.
Privacy/compliance implications of each approach were only partially covered.
assumptions: 'Support agent' refers to a conversational AI system handling multi-turn customer or employee support interactions.
Comparisons reflect the state of the field as of early-to-mid 2026.
Production suitability is assessed for enterprise-scale deployments, not just prototypes.
next_steps: Evaluate memory frameworks head-to-head using benchmarks like MemGPT, Zep, A-Mem, and Mem0 (as in the 'Hindsight is 20/20' arXiv study).
Assess data governance readiness before selecting an approach — ungoverned data degrades all retrieval-based methods.
Prototype a hybrid architecture (hot/warm/cold tiering) and measure latency and accuracy against your specific support query distribution.
Consider privacy and compliance requirements (e.g., GDPR right-to-erasure) when choosing between vector stores, knowledge graphs, and parametric approaches.

flagged_claims: In-context memory is volatile, lost at session end, and has zero retrieval latency
Vector store memory retrieves by cosine similarity and adds 50-200ms per retrieval
Summarization approach uses rolling summaries and async consolidation to cap storage growth
Most production agentic systems in 2026 use both RAG and memory (hybrid approach)
judge_verdict.reasoning: Most of the draft is supported by the sources, including the multi-turn performance drop, RAG accuracy ranges, fine-tuning trade-offs, bi-temporal knowledge graphs, and hybrid convergence, but a few specific latency and implementation details are not directly evidenced.
